Guidelines for the NRT Home Study Training Module Please note that this home study training course is intended to replace four live seminar days. Study and practice accordingly. 1. There was a tremendous amount of material covered in the Advanced NRT live course with no time to read the extensive (153 page) manual or to review and 
practice the 80+ techniques and new applications of NRT. Now is the time. 2. Review and practice the ten ways to Maximize Success with the Reset. 3. Review the section, How Does One Distinguish Between Trigger Points in Muscle Tissue and Trigger Points in Ligaments, Periosteum, And Tendons? And the 
associated materials in the succeeding pages. 4. Review and practice the material covered in Applying NRT to Tendons, Ligaments, and the Periosteum of Bones with a Reflex Hammer. 5. Review and practice the material covered in Applying NRT to Tendons and Joint Capsular Ligaments Using the Direction of Ease. 6. Review and practice Sports Specific Resets and the NRT Universal Reset Technique. 7. Read and study the section: Why Can't We Do NRT on Ourselves – the expanded answer. 8. Read and study each technique in the remaining part of the manual for: a. How to Reset 23 More Muscles b. 16 Expanded, Revised, and New Techniques for the Muscles You Already Know c. How to Create Resets for Any Muscle (plus 6 new muscles) d. Miraculous Sequence We will study and practice each zone and/or muscle like we did in the live Advanced

text:
How Safe Are the Massager Units on the Head and Neck?  When I first started using the massager units, more specifically the Hitachi designed Magic Wands, I was concerned that the vibration applied to the head or neck regions 
might injure the brain or other tissues. 
 
 After reviewing research done on the considerable amount of G forces on the human brain during such common human activities as running, I was far less concerned. 
 
 Whole body vibration training has been used safely by people for many years now. This training takes place as a person stands on a metal plate that vibrates very 
intensely. 
 
 The amount of G force on a fighter pilot's brain may be 500 times that produced by a whole-body vibration machine. 
 
 In a 2013 study done in China on 44 elderly and clinically frail patients between the ages of 82 and 89, vibration training done at twice the usual force, generated nothing 
but positive results. In fact, 15, out of the 19 who could not get around without 
wheelchairs before the training, could now stand and walk without assistance. 
 
 The amount of G forces from the massager units is far less than what is generated and absorbed into the body from the whole-body vibration machines. The amount of time 
on any one area is also just a fraction of what is experienced during vibration training. 
 
 For decades, many people did a type of passive exercise by leaning back on a wide belt connected to a motor that shook them violently without any apparent harm. 
 
 Still there are precautions – see the next page. 7
section: 7. Read and study the section: Why Can't We Do NRT on Ourselves – the expanded
primary_use: Evaluating safety of massager units on head and neck

text:
Contraindications 1. Clients with a history of easy bruising - not an absolute contraindication, be cautious. 2. Clients with osteoporosis - again, be cautious. 3. Clients with a history of physical or psychological trauma - no treatment without full 
consent from the Client. Even with consent, if the patient's psychological demeanor changes 
after a reset, you may have to discontinue treatment. 4. Clients with unstable joints or joint replacements - the therapist should take caution on 
which angles of application and the amount of force that the joints can tolerate without 
dislocation or traumatization. The clients may have to alter some of the positions and 
movement patterns that are described in the techniques. 5. Clients with a history of recent surgery, unhealed sprains and strains, or active 
inflammation - do not apply the massager units on or stress those particular areas. 6. Clients with an active inflammatory process of their joints such as rheumatoid arthritis – 
avoid any direct work of the areas until they are in an inactive phase. 7. No electromechanical massage around the thyroid gland or near the eyes (especially any 
type of retinal disorders), or abdominal areas of pregnant women. 8. Electromechanical massage is not to be applied to the posterior calf in clients who may be 
subject to deep vein thrombosis or on any areas of acute thrombosis. 9. Some electromechanical units may require padding or using the tips of the fingers while 
grasping the balls of the units before using directly on the head or the anterior and lateral 
areas of the neck. 10. Epilepsy, severe migraines, cancer, unhealed closed head trauma, pacemakers or other 
electronic implanted devices may forbid the use of massager units - or at least be locally 
contraindicated. 11. Avoid any electromechanical massage on areas under current therapy with Botox® 
injections. 12. Any other conditions that are contraindications for manual therapy applications which 
you learned during your basic training as a therapist. 8
section: 2. Clients with osteoporosis - again, be cautious.
primary_use: This chunk lists contraindications for NRT treatment, which is essential for practitioners to know before applying the therapy.

text: Everything Reset (ER) Sequence Always palpate the whole body before and after doing the ERS! ❖ This particular sequence started manifesting itself as I saw what kind of truly amazing results that therapists were having with the Miraculous Sequence (MS). Not all muscles were addressed in the Miraculous Sequence. This new Everything Reset Sequence (ERS) resets many muscles not done in the MS or even taught in the previous class modules. ❖ This sequence resets 321 muscles – 160 pairs, plus the respiratory diaphragm. ❖ Almost of the Everything Reset Sequence is done with the use of Twin resets, mostly bilateral, a few unilateral. There is one unilateral Indirect reset. ❖ All of the resets are done with the therapists using the massager units. A trained 10- year-old could easily treat a 400-pound football player effectively. ❖ The heavy use of the bilateral Twin reset with the Client actively contracting the muscles as the therapist is electromassaging on the twin muscles creates a powerful reset mechanism while decreasing the amount of work and effort that the therapist has to do. ❖ Unlike the Miraculous Sequence, all of the resets are done with the Client sitting and lying on a table or a floor mat. This is easier for the Client to move in various ways. Also, this provides a more traditional setting for the Client. ❖ Like the Miraculous Sequence, the basic principle of applying NRT in the Miraculous Sequence is to reset the more superficial muscles first for an area and then reset the deeper ones whenever possible. ❖ Like the Miraculous Sequence, the resets are coordinated to create a flow that moves quickly from one to the next. How long to do all of the ERS? – as short as 21 minutes. Try to get your time down to 45 minutes. ❖ One of the upsides of the ERS is that it is done in positions where it is easy to palpate for indications of the need for a reset. 9
section: 2. Clients with osteoporosis - again, be cautious.
primary_use: describes a treatment sequence for therapists

text: ❖ When you see the results from the ERS, you will reserve the MS for those situations in which you are short on time, need time reserved for other types of procedures that day, do not have a table, or do not have/cannot use the massager units. Important Notes for the Therapist Before Beginning the Everything Reset Sequence 1. Conduct a thorough health history and review for contraindications to NRT work. 2. Palpate the whole body to give the Client an education of what tight, tender, or ticklish areas currently exist in their body. Do not skip this step, especially before doing the ERS. This sequence is so non-invasive and unique from the usual forms of manual therapy your clients have experienced in the past; they will not believe that it did much. 3. Do the whole body before palpating again. 4. For the problematic areas, spend a little more time and be more thorough with the application of the massager units to all of the involved muscles. 5. Do not forget about tweaking. You may have your clients move: In a fuller range of motion, a little faster or slower, for a longer time, with eyes closed, or in a somewhat different pattern than originally done You may apply the stimulus: More erratically, for a longer time, focus on the tendons, or use your hands or hammers 6. Palpate again and have the Client acknowledge the positive effects of this "crazy" therapy. 7. For any remaining issues, you may choose to use a basic level or Miraculous Sequence technique to quickly clear the area or you may repeat a specific ERS step. 10
primary_use: Provides clinical perspective on NRT, its applications and considerations for therapists.

text:
Abdominals (External and Internal Obliques) Resets A. Position: Client is sitting and hugs herself. Trunk is comfortably rotated (not laterally flexed) in either 
direction. Some female clients will need to lift up the breast tissue off the ribs with their 
forearms. Therapist stands facing Client while holding the massager units. A. Action: Client oscillates near the end range of motion towards one side as the therapist uses 
massager units on the Abdominal Obliques (p. 210 – 211) on the lower ribs. Repeat on parallel levels until reaching the rim of the pelvic bone and inguinal ligament 
attachments. B. Position and Action: Repeat with trunk rotated in the opposite direction. C. Position: Client is sitting, hugs herself, and leans to right (lateral flexion of the spine). Therapist stands facing Client with one hand holding a massager unit on the left side. C. Action: Client holds the position leaning to the right and moving a little while the therapist applies 
the massager unit on the left lateral fibers of the Abdominal Obliques. D. Position and Action: Reverse and repeat procedure on the other side. 14
primary_use: Muscle anatomy and treatment protocol for Abdominals (External and Internal Obliques)

text:
Abdominals (Rectus Abdominis, Pyramidalis) Resets Position: Client is sitting, leans back some, and hugs herself. Some female clients will need to lift the 
breast tissue off their ribs with their forearms. Therapist is standing facing the Client while holding the massager units. Therapist may anchor 
Client to the table by pressing a thigh against a knee of the Client. Action: Client is repeatedly rocking forward (spinal flexion) as the therapist applies the massager 
units on the Rectus Abdominis and Pyramidalis (p. 209 – 212) muscles from the superior edge 
of the pubic bones to and including the attachments on the anterior ribcage up to the fifth 
rib. Start Position 
Bilateral Twin Reset 
Treatment Area 16
primary_use: This chunk describes a treatment protocol for bilateral twin reset, including anatomical structures and action steps.

text:
Abdominals (Transverse) Reset Note: Do this reset after resetting all the more superficial muscles, the Rectus Abdominis and 
the Internal and External Obliques . Contraindications: abdominal herniations, acute abdominal inflammatory processes, and 
clients on anticoagulant medications (check with doctor if electromassage may be ok with the 
meds). Position: Client is sitting, hunched forwards while holding shoulders. Therapist is standing in front of 
the Client with the massager units close to the Linea Alba at the midline of the trunk. Overweight clients should sit straight and reach for the ceiling. Action: Client repeatedly pulls the navel towards the spine (creating abdominal compression). Therapist applies the massager units on both sides simultaneously of the Transverse 
Abdominis (p. 211) muscles starting near the Linea Alba and moving posteriorly. Be sure to 
stimulate on the portion of the Transverse Abdominis inferior to the anterior rib cage. Start Position 
Bilateral Twin Reset Treatment Area 
Includes Part of the Back 17
primary_use: provides anatomical information and a treatment protocol for resetting the Transverse Abdominis muscle

text:
Intercostals (External and Internal)/Respiratory Diaphragm Resets Position: Client is sitting with arm lifted out of the way (may lift breast tissue, if necessary). Therapist is holding the massager units near the anterior and posterior surfaces of the left rib 
cage. Action: Client is breathing in and out repeatedly (deep but not rapid) as the therapist applies the 
massager units on each segment of the left rib cage (p. 213 – 215). Avoid female breast tissue 
by applying massager units on the anterolateral and posterior rib cage at those segments. Repeat on the right rib cage to reset the left Intercostals and Respiratory Diaphragm. Unilateral Twin Reset 18
primary_use: Resets the Intercostals and Respiratory Diaphragm position by applying massager units on each segment of the rib cage, promoting deep breathing.

text:
Serratus Posterior Superior and Serratus Posterior Inferior Resets Position: Client is sitting with shoulders hunched forward (to further expose the muscles). Therapist is behind the Client while holding the massager units close to the Serratus Posterior 
Superior (p. 216) muscles which lie deep to the more superficial Rhomboids. Actions: A. Client inhales into the upper back as the therapist applies electromassage to the Serratus 
Posterior Superior muscles. B. As Client exhales from the lower thoracic/upper lumbar area, the therapist applies 
electromassage to the Serratus Posterior Inferior (p. 216) muscles that run diagonally from 
the spinous processes of T12 – L3 to the posterior of ribs 9 – 12. Repeat for several cycles of breathing. Start Position 
Bilateral Twin Reset 
Treatment Area 19
primary_use: Client resets position of Serratus Posterior Superior and Inferior muscles via electromassage

text:
Latissimus Dorsi/Teres Major Reset – Part A Position: Client is sitting with the elbows straight, and the backs of the wrists against the lateral side of 
the thighs. Therapist is standing behind the Client while holding the massager units. Action: Client attempts to repeatedly roll wrists into further medial rotation while also pushing 
against lateral thighs and down into the table (to medially rotate, extend and adduct 
shoulders) as the therapist applies electromassage over the Latissimus Dorsi, Teres Major, 
and the thoracolumbar aponeurosis (p. 71 – 73). Start Position 
Bilateral Twin Reset 
Treatment Area 20
primary_use: A muscle anatomy description with treatment protocol information to reset the Latissimus Dorsi and Teres Major muscles.

text:
Platysma Reset Position: Client is sitting. Therapist is holding the massager units. Action: Client repeatedly grimaces to activate the Platysma (p. 257) muscles as the therapist applies 
the stimulation to them from the sides of the mouth, over the mandible, and over the 
superior portion of Pectoralis Major. Avoid the thyroid area. Apply very lightly and superficially as the muscles lie just under the skin. Start Position 
Bilateral Twin Reset 
Treatment Area 21
primary_use: Reset position instructions for Platysma muscle treatment

text:
Pectoralis Major/Sternalis Resets Position: Client is sitting with palms together and fingers interlaced. Elbows are bent about 90° and 
arms parallel to the floor. Therapist is facing the Client and holds massager units close to the Client's chest. Action: Client compresses palms together continuously while moving arms in an arc, oscillating 
between above and below the head and later pushing into lower abdomen. For male clients, therapist stimulates the Pectoralis Major/Sternalis (p. 88 – 91) muscles. Stimulate more on the upper fibers when arms are above the head and stimulate more on 
the lower fibers when arms are closer to or are pressing into the abdominal area. For female clients, electromassage the muscle near the arm and clavicle and if possible, on 
the sternal and costal attachments. 22
primary_use: Muscle reset position for Pectoralis Major/Sternalis

text:
Start Position 
Bilateral Twin Reset 
End Position Treatment Area Treatment Area For Female For Male 23
primary_use: Bilateral Twin Reset is a treatment area for both male and female, requiring knowledge of anatomy and tissue involvement.

text:
Pectoralis Minor Reset Position: Client is sitting. Therapist is facing the Client while holding the massager units. Action: Client repeatedly hunches shoulders forward and down (scapulae protracted and depressed) 
as the therapist stimulates the Pectoralis Minor (p. 91 – 93) muscles, except for the portion 
with breast tissue. End Position 
Bilateral Twin Reset 
Treatment Area 24
primary_use: Muscle reset position protocol for Pectoralis Minor

text:
Subclavius Reset Position: Client is sitting. Therapist is holding the massager units. Action: Client repeatedly depresses shoulders and brings them forward while inhaling into the upper 
chest (to depress the clavicle and bring it anteriorly while elevating the first rib) as the 
therapist electromassages the Subclavian (p. 94) muscles located under the inferior border of 
the clavicle. End Position 
Bilateral Twin Reset 
Treatment Area 25
primary_use: Describes a treatment position and protocol for Subclavius Reset

text:
Serratus Anterior Reset Position: Client is sitting with upper extremities pointed in front with elbows straight and thumbs 
pointed towards the ceiling. Therapist is standing facing the Client while holding the massager units. Action: Client is to repeatedly move shoulders towards the therapist, always keeping shoulders 
depressed, while moving hands so the thumbs point towards the floor (scapulae into 
abduction, upwardly rotation, and depression) while the therapist electromassages the 
accessible portion of the Serratus Anterior (p. 86 – 88) muscles on the anterolateral ribcage. Start Position 
Bilateral Twin Reset End Position and Treatment Area 26
primary_use: NRT treatment protocol for Serratus Anterior muscle reset

text:
Trapezius (Lower) Reset Position: Client is sitting with trunk leaning forward. Hands are near the lateral side of knees with the 
elbows straight. Therapist faces the back of the Client while holding the massager units. Action: Client lifts up and separates the upper extremities (up and away) repeatedly (to produce 
scapular depression and upwardly rotation) as the therapist applies electromassage on the 
Lower Trapezius (p. 68 – 70) muscles. End Position 
Start Position 
Bilateral Twin Reset Treatment Area 27
primary_use: Treatment protocol for Lower Trapezius muscle reset

text:
Trapezius (Middle)/Rhomboids Major and Minor Resets A. Position: Client is sitting while grasping the backs of elbows. Therapist faces the back of Client and is holding the massager units. B. Position: The same as A, except that the Client is grasping forearms near the wrist. C. Position: Same as A, except that the Client has tips of curled fingers locked together. Action: Client attempts to pull apart the locked arms (scapular adduction) while repeatedly lifting the 
elbows up and down as the therapist stimulates the Middle Trapezii and Rhomboids (p. 68 – 
70, 82-83). 28
primary_use: Resets Middle Trapezius/Rhomboids Major and Minor using massager units.

text:
Position A 
Bilateral Twin Reset 
Position B Position C 
Treatment Area 29
primary_use: Muscle anatomy and treatment protocol information for bilateral twin reset.

text:
Trapezius (Upper)/Levator Scapula Resets Position: Client is sitting with scapulae anchored in a depressed position by sliding hands underneath 
buttocks (palms up). The neck is comfortably flexed. The trunk is leaning forwards a little. Therapist is standing behind the Client while holding the massager units. Actions: A. Client repeatedly tries to bring the points of shoulders and ears together (to attempt to 
elevate and upwardly rotate the scapulae) as the therapist stimulates all of the upper 
Trapezius (p. 68 – 69) fibers including the ones on the posterior neck. B. Repeat same actions and stimulate the Levator Scapulae (p. 83 – 85) tendon attachments 
on the superior angle and medial border of the scapula and on the upper lateral neck. C. Repeat the same actions as in A. with the Client extending the neck as they are trying to 
bring the points of their shoulders and their ears together. D. Client elevates their shoulders and keeps them up while moving their shoulders forwards 
and backwards as the therapist applies the stimulus to the fibers of the upper Trapezius. Note: Make sure client anchors the shoulders well before they attempt to elevate them. A 
tweak would have the Client not anchor the shoulders and moving the shoulders up and 
down in a greater range of motion. 30
primary_use: Positional relaxation and muscle reset treatment for scapular depression

text:
End Position  
Bilateral Twin Reset Treatment Area 
For Trapezius (Upper) Treatment Area

text:
Erector Spinae/ Intertransversarii/Interspinalis (Lumbar) Resets Position: Client is sitting while leaning forwards comfortably from the hips. If needed, Client may use 
hands against their thighs or an object to partially alleviate some of the weight. Therapist stands behind the Client while holding the massager units. Action: Client repeatedly leans backwards (lumbar spinal extension) from the hips a little while the 
therapist stimulates simultaneously on the Erector Spinae (p. 196 – 199) on both sides. Since 
the Erector Spinae are segmentally innervated, it is important to stimulate each level on both 
sides at the same time. Be sure to include the sacrum also. Remember that there are three strips (Spinalis, Longissimus, and Iliocostalis) of the Erector 
Spinae on either side so stimulate a fairly broad area. Coordinate your stimulation so that you 
are touching the same strip of the Erector Spinae on both sides simultaneously. Start Position

lateral portion of the posterior thigh. Start and End Position Bilateral Twin Reset Treatment Area 88

– 331) which mostly comprise the inferior two-thirds of the buttocks. Action: Client repeatedly crosses over ankles/legs in a wide arc (laterally rotating their hips) as the

Gluteus Medius, Gluteus Minimus, and Tensor Fascia Latae (p. 315 – 318, 324 – 325) which 
mostly comprise the superior one-third of the buttocks and the lateral side of the pelvis. Action: Client repeatedly moves feet away from each other (medially rotating their hips) as the

Popliteus Reset Position: Client is lying face down with knees bent enough to allow the feet to move freely. Therapist is holding the massager units. Action: Client repeatedly rotates legs inwardly (creating medial rotation of the knee)) as therapist 
stimulates the bulk of the Popliteus (p. 375) on the medial side of the leg, on the first few 
inches (1-5 cm) just below the knee. Stay away from the popliteal fossa (the pit behind the 
knee). End Position

Action: Client repeatedly presses the backs of arms into the end of the table while also attempting to 
move them towards each other (shoulder extension and adduction) as the therapist applies 
electromassage over the Latissimus Dorsi, Teres Major, and the thoracolumbar aponeurosis

Foot (Evertors) Resets Position: Client is supine with the lower extremities rotated medially. Therapist grasps one foot and demonstrates the required movement of foot eversion and 
then holds the massager units. Action: Client repeatedly everts feet as the therapist stimulates the tendons and muscle fibers of 
Peroneus Longus, Peroneus Brevis, and Extensor Digitorum Longus (p. 376 – 380) located on 
the top of the foot (except for the great toe), around the top and bottom of the lateral 
malleolus (outside ankle), and in the anterior calf. End Position  
Start Position

text:
Foot (Invertors) Resets Position: Client is supine with the lower extremities rotated laterally. Therapist grasps one foot and demonstrates the required movement of foot inversion and 
then holds the massager units. Action: Client repeatedly inverts feet as the therapist stimulates the tendons of Tibialis Posterior, 
Flexor Digitorum Longus, and Flexor Hallucis Longus (p. 381 – 383) located on the bottom of 
the feet, around the lateral border of the medial malleolus (inside ankle) and then superior 
several inches (1-7 cm) along the posterior border of the tibia. Also, the therapist stimulates 
the Tibialis Anterior and Extensor Hallucis Longus (p. 378 – 380) in the anterior calf. Start Position

Ankles (Plantar Flexors) and Toes (Flexors)/                                      
Ankles (Dorsiflexors) and Toes (Extensors) Resets Position: Client is lying face up. Therapist is standing, facing the Client's feet, with hands holding the massager units. A. Action: Client repeatedly moves ankles downward (into plantar flexion) and toes curled down (into 
flexion) as the therapist applies the massager units to the bottom of the feet, including the 
toes (p. 384 – 391). The stimulation is applied there to the tendons of Tibialis Posterior that plantar flex the 
ankles and of Flexor Digitorum Longus and Flexor Hallucis Longus (p.381-383) that plantar flex 
the ankles and flex the toes. Also located there are the toe flexors: Flexor Digitorum Brevis, 
Abductor Hallucis, Abductor Digiti Minimi, Flexor Hallucis Brevis, Adductor Hallucis, Flexor 
Digiti Minimi Brevis, Quadratus Plantae, Plantar Interossei, and the Lumbricals (p. 384-391). B. Action: Client repeatedly moves ankles upward (into dorsiflexion) and toes towards the head (into 
extension) as the therapist applies the massager units to the top of the feet, including the 
toes (p. 384 – 391), and to the anterior calves. The stimulation is applied there to Tibialis Anterior, Peroneus Longus and Brevis that dorsiflex 
the ankle and to the Extensor Digitorum Longus and Extensor Hallucis Longus (p.378-380) that 
dorsiflex the ankle and extend the toes. Also located there are the toe extensors: Extensor 
Digitorum Brevis and Extensor Hallucis Brevis (p. 387, 390). 98

three areas: 1. The top (dorsum) of the feet for the Dorsal Interossei (p. 391). 2. The lateral part of the bottom of the feet from the little toe to the heel for the Abductor

Sartorius Reset A. Position: Client is lying face up (supine) with knees bent with feet on the table and spread apart. Therapist demonstrates the desired motion by moving the Client's left lower extremity so

text:
Scalenes (Anterior) Reset (Optional Method) Warning: Unless you know your anatomy very well and your palpation skills are excellent, it 
will be safer to perform the Anterior Scalenes reset taught in the basic upper body class. Position: Client is lying supine (face up). Therapist first palpates for the anterior tubercles (p. 179 – 181) of the transverse processes of 
C3 – C5. This is where the origins of the Anterior Scalenes (p. 246 – 249) are attached. Note: 
the experienced therapist will know that there is a very prominent anterior tubercle at C6 
(Chassaignac tubercle). After locating the target zone, the therapist will hold the massager 
units by their heads, not their handles, and inserts middle three fingertips (palms up) under 
the lateral borders of the Sternocleidomastoids (p. 244 – 245) at the C3-C5 vertebral levels. Action: Client repeatedly lifts up head/neck (to flex neck) while inhaling as the therapist very lightly 
presses onto the attachments of the Anterior Scalenes on the anterior tubercles with the 
backs of (very short) fingernails at the C3-C5 vertebral levels. Please note that the inhalation is critical for the success of this reset. Start Position  
Bilateral Twin Reset 110

Tongue (Protractor, Retractor, Elevator, Depressor) Resets Position: Client is lying supine (face up). Therapist is holding the massager units by their heads, not their handles. The therapist places 
fingertips under the mandible and lightly presses into the space between the posterior side of 
the mandible and the superior border of the hyoid bone (p. 254). Press randomly. Note: The muscles involved with these movements of the tongue are not covered in the 
anatomy book for this course. Actions: Client is instructed to do the following actions: 1. Repeatedly stick out tongue fully (protraction) – Genioglossus 2. Repeatedly pull it back into mouth fully (retraction) – Styloglossus 3. Repeatedly press the top of tongue against the roof (palate) of mouth (elevation) – 
Styloglossus and Palatoglossus 4. Repeatedly press the base of tongue into the floor of mouth (depression) – Hyoglossus The Left Side of the Treatment

Mandible (Depressors) Resets Position: Client is lying supine (face up). Therapist is holding the massager units by their heads, not their handles. A. Action: Client is instructed to start with the mandible half-way open and then move into fully open

thyroid glands. Apply very lightly and superficially as the muscles lie just under the skin. 114

text:
Negative Facial Expressions Resets Note: Because it would be difficult to instruct clients to contract just one side of the face, 
these are all addressed by the use of a Bilateral Twin Reset. Of course, if one side cannot be 
stimulated directly due to injury or paralysis, then do a Unilateral Twin Reset. These muscles 
are very superficial. Avoid these resets on botoxed clients. These resets are valuable to facial 
muscles that remain tender long after suffering a physical trauma. Another reason to reset these muscles is to affect the psychology of the Client. Research 
shows that if a person even dons a fake smile, the mood of the person starts to change for 
the better. As discussed in the advanced class, the psychology of the person drives the 
physiology through alterations of the balance between the sympathetic and the 
parasympathetic nervous systems. If your Client is stuck in a negative emotional state 
because of stress, these resets may help them relax and begin to heal. Position for all: Client is sitting or lying supine. Therapist has fingertips extended from hands cupped around the ball ends of massager units 
poised above the muscles. Action for all: Client demonstrates the mood requested by the therapist. The more the Client exaggerates 
the expression, the better. The therapist stimulates on both sides simultaneously with 
fingertips or pads transmitting the vibration of the massager units. Be Gentle! Avoid the eyes completely. 124
section: 2. Repeatedly pull it back into mouth fully (retraction) – Styloglossus
primary_use: Resetting facial muscles to affect physiology and psychology
